title: Field Theory of Data: Anomaly Detection via the Functional Renormalization Group. The 2D Ising Model as a Benchmark
authors: Riccardo Finotello, Vincent Lahoche, Parham Radpay, Dine Ousmane Samary
abstract: We establish a correspondence between anomaly detection in high-noise regimes and the renormalization group flow of non-equilibrium field theories. We provide a physical grounding for this framework by proving that the detection of phase transitions in interacting non-equilibrium systems maps to the study of an effective equilibrium field theory near its Gaussian fixed point, which we identify with the universal Marchenko-Pastur distribution. Applying the Functional Renormalization Group to the two-dimensional Model A, we demonstrate that the noise-to-signal ratio acts as a physical temperature, where the signal emerges as ordered domains within a thermalized background of fluctuations. Using the exact Onsager solution as a benchmark, we show that this approach identifies critical thresholds with an error below 4%, significantly outperforming standard information-theoretic metrics such as the Kullback-Leibler divergence. Our results provide a universal strategy for resolving structures in complex datasets near criticality, bridging the gap between statistical mechanics and statistical inference.
summary: This paper proposes a new strategy for detecting signals in noisy data by treating the problem as a phase transition in a non-equilibrium field theory. By using the Functional Renormalization Group, the authors show that one can identify the emergence of signal from noise by monitoring the relevance of physical couplings. This approach provides a more precise method for resolving structures in complex datasets compared to traditional statistical methods.
main_problem: Developing a robust method for anomaly detection in high-noise datasets by bridging the gap between statistical inference and non-equilibrium phase transitions.
main_result: The authors establish that signal detection can be framed as identifying phase transitions in field theories, demonstrating that the Functional Renormalization Group (FRG) can identify critical thresholds with less than 4% error, outperforming KL divergence.
method: The study uses the Functional Renormalization Group (FRG) with the Wetterich-Morris equation and Local Potential Approximation (LPA) to track the flow of interaction couplings.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to a 2D Ising model (benchmark) and a continuous Model A (Langevin dynamics) governed by a time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau Hamiltonian with a quartic potential.
key_observables: Canonical dimensions of couplings (u2, u4, u6), Binder cumulant, eigenvalue spectrum, and noise-to-signal ratio (NSR).
important_parameters: Temperature (analogous to NSR), coupling constants (b), and the aspect ratio of the data matrix (q).
main_assumptions: The approach is limited to analyzing flow around the Gaussian fixed point and uses the Hartree approximation, which is insufficient to reach the exact Onsager limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the difficulty of separating heavy-tailed signals from noise, the behavior of coupling dimensions as NSR decreases, and a comparison of critical temperature estimates using Binder cumulants and GSA.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the correspondence between anomaly detection and RG flow, establishes the mathematical mapping to effective field theory, presents numerical simulations using the 2D Ising model and Model A, and concludes with a performance comparison against standard information-theoretic metrics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2.1. Numerical simulation of a quench for N = 100: starting at t = 0 at a very high temperature, the spins are plunged into a thermal bath below the critical temperature. The left image shows step 0, and the next two images correspond to time steps 3 × 103 and 39 × 103.
Figure 3.1. Canonical dimensions for a random matrix X ∈RN×P , where N = 4×104, and P/N = 0.5, plotted against the interpolation of the eigenvalue spectrum.

Figure 3.2. Behavior of the canonical dimension by decreasing the NSR from higher values (top left), to intermediate values (top right), to a pure MP contribution (bottom). Data extracted from our previous work [8].

Figure 4.1. Behavior of the spectrum found by the KL-proxy for high temperature, T = 5 (top left), in the vicinity of the critical regime T = 0.648 (top right) and below the critical temperature T = 0.350 (bottom) for b = 0.8.
Figure 4.2. Behavior of the Binder cumulant for different grid sizes with b = 0.4 (left). Empirical behavior of λc for different temperatures with different methods for b = 0.8 (right).
